metadata.summary: pytest-mock's mocker fixture wraps unittest.mock. Covers patch targets, autospec, spy, async mocking, side effects, call verification, and antipatterns like over-mocking and wrong patch path.

content.title: pytest Mocking — mocker Fixture, Autospec, Spy, and Async Mocks
content.summary: Use pytest-mock's mocker fixture instead of unittest.mock directly. The mocker fixture auto-cleans up patches after each test, supports autospec for signature validation, provides AsyncMock for async callables, and exposes a spy() helper that calls through to the real implementation while recording calls.

why.p: unittest.mock used with context managers or decorators requires manual cleanup and produces verbose test code. pytest-mock's mocker fixture integrates with pytest's fixture lifecycle so all patches are reverted automatically after each test, even on failure. autospec=True creates a mock whose signature mirrors the real object, catching call-site drift immediately rather than at production runtime. AsyncMock replaces regular Mock for async callables — a regular Mock returns a non-awaitable and the test passes silently while asserting nothing about async behavior.

list.item: Isolating a unit from external dependencies: HTTP APIs, email services, databases, file systems.
Testing error paths that are hard to trigger naturally: network timeouts, disk full, auth failures.
Verifying that a dependency is called with the correct arguments.
Controlling non-deterministic behavior: time, random values, UUID generation.
Replacing async callables in async test code.
Over-mocking: mocking every collaborator so the test only tests the mock configuration, not real behavior.
Mocking internal private methods — tests become coupled to implementation details and break on refactoring.
Replacing a database with mocks in integration tests — use a real test database with transactions and rollback instead.
Tests where testcontainers or a real service is available — mocks diverge from real API contracts over time.

rule.statement: Always patch the name where it is USED (imported into), not where it is DEFINED. If module_a imports helper_function from module_b and you want to intercept the call in module_a, patch "module_a.helper_function", not "module_b.helper_function".
rule.rationale: When Python executes "from module_b import helper_function", it binds the name helper_function in module_a's namespace. Patching module_b.helper_function replaces the original but module_a.helper_function still points to the old binding. Patching where it is used replaces the binding that the code under test actually calls.

example.title: Patching where defined — does not work
Patching where used — works correctly
example.code: # test_a.py — CORRECT def test_good_mock_target(mocker): mocker.patch("module_a.helper_function", return_value="mocked") result = my_function() assert result == "mocked"

example.title: Mocking external HTTP API with verification
example.code:
def test_fetch_weather_data(mocker): mock_response = mocker.Mock() mock_response.json.return_value = { "temperature": 22, "conditions": "sunny", } mock_response.status_code = 200
mocker.patch("requests.get", return_value=mock_response)
result = WeatherService.get_current_weather("London")
assert result["temperature"] == 22 assert result["conditions"] == "sunny"
def test_send_notification_calls_email_service(mocker): mock_send = mocker.patch("app.services.email_client.send") mock_send.return_value = {"status": "sent", "id": "123"}
result = NotificationService.send_notification(user_id=1, message="Hello!")
assert result["status"] == "sent" mock_send.assert_called_once_with( to="user1@example.com", subject="Notification", body="Hello!", )

example.title: Side effects: exception and successive return values
example.code:
def test_fetch_weather_handles_api_error(mocker): mocker.patch( "requests.get", side_effect=requests.RequestException("Connection failed"), )
with pytest.raises(WeatherServiceError) as exc_info: WeatherService.get_current_weather("London")
assert "Unable to fetch weather data" in str(exc_info.value)
def test_retry_succeeds_on_second_attempt(mocker): # First call raises, second call returns data mocker.patch( "requests.get", side_effect=[ requests.ConnectionError("Timeout"), mocker.Mock(status_code=200, json=lambda: {"ok": True}), ], ) result = fetch_with_retry("https://api.example.com/data") assert result["ok"] is True

section.name: autospec
section.p: autospec=True creates a mock that mirrors the real object's signature. Calling the mock with wrong arguments raises TypeError immediately, catching drift between test mocks and production API.

rule.statement: Use mocker.patch.object(cls, "method", autospec=True) or mocker.create_autospec(RealClass) when mocking non-trivial collaborators. Never use a plain mocker.Mock() for objects whose interface you do not own or that evolve frequently.
rule.rationale: A plain Mock accepts any call signature. If the real method's signature changes, the mock silently accepts the old call and the test keeps passing while production code breaks. autospec catches the mismatch at test time.

example.title: autospec prevents signature drift

section.name: spy-pattern
section.p: mocker.spy() wraps a real callable, records calls, but still calls through to the original implementation. Use it when you need to verify behavior without replacing it.

example.title: Spy on logger without replacing it

section.name: async-mocking
section.p: Regular Mock returns a non-awaitable. When mocking async callables use mocker.AsyncMock (or new_callable=mocker.AsyncMock). Verify awaits with assert_awaited_once() rather than assert_called_once().

example.title: AsyncMock for async external service
example.note: assert_awaited_once() checks the coroutine was both called and awaited. assert_called_once() only checks the call.

antipattern.description: Using mocker.Mock() (not AsyncMock) for an async callable in an async test.
antipattern.reason: A plain Mock() returns a non-awaitable object. The test passes because await mock() evaluates to Mock.__await__() returning the mock itself — silently asserting nothing about the async call.

antipattern.description: Mocking every collaborator of the unit under test including internal validation, duplicate checking, persistence, and notification — leaving the test only verifying that the mocks were called.
antipattern.reason: A test with more mock setup than real assertion tests only the mock configuration, not the production code path. If the service's logic is wrong, this test passes. Mock only external dependencies (network, disk, time); let the production code run for everything inside the service boundary.

example.title: Over-mocked — tests nothing real
example.code:
def test_user_service_over_mocked(mocker): mocker.patch.object(UserService, "validate_email", return_value=True) mocker.patch.object(UserService, "check_duplicate", return_value=False) mocker.patch.object(UserRepository, "save", return_value=User(id=1)) mocker.patch.object(EmailService, "send_welcome", return_value=True)
result = UserService.register(email="test@example.com") assert result.id == 1 # only tests that the mock returned id=1

example.title: Mock only external dependency — real logic runs

checklist.check: Verify mock was called (assert_called_once_with / assert_called_with) when the call itself is the behavior under test.
Check call arguments when argument correctness matters.
Use assert_awaited_once() (not assert_called_once()) for async mocks.
Use autospec=True for collaborators whose interface evolves.
Patch at the usage location, not the definition.
Avoid mocking internal methods — test through the public interface.
Prefer mocker.patch() over unittest.mock.patch as a decorator — auto-cleanup prevents fixture ordering bugs.

list.item: Patching module_b.helper_function instead of module_a.helper_function — the usage binding is what matters.
Using Mock() for an async callable — it silently passes; the test asserts nothing. Always AsyncMock for coroutines.
mocker.patch.object without autospec — the mock accepts any call, hiding signature drift.
Forgetting that monkeypatch.setenv does not reload modules that read env at import time — the change is invisible.
pytest-recording / vcrpy cassettes committed with secrets baked in — always use filter_headers before committing cassettes.
